IN THE HIGH COURT OF LESOTHO
HELD AT MASERU                                 CONST CASE NO.0013/2024
In the matter between:
HONOURABLE TEBOHO MOJAPELA                    1
ST
APPLICANT
BASOTHO COVENANT MOVEMENT (BCM)          2
ND
APPLICANT
POPULAR FRONT FOR DEMOCRACY (PFD)         3
RD
APPLICANT
AND
THE HON. SPEAKER OF THE NATIONAL
ASSEMBLY                                                                 1
ST
RESPONDENT
THE HON. LEADER OF THE HOUSE OF
PARLIAMENT                                                            2
ND
RESPONDENT
THE HON. PRIME MINISTER OF LESOTHO          3
RD
RESPONDENT
THE HON. MINISTER OF LAW AND CONSTI-
TUTIONAL AFFAIRS                                                 4
TH
RESPONDENT
THE ATTORNEY GENERAL                                    5
TH
RESPONDENT
Neutral Citation:
Hon. Teboho Mojapela & 2 Others v The Hon. Speaker of the National Assembly & 4 Others
[2026] LSHC 44
Const. (05 MARCH 2026)
CORAM:             MOKHESI J
MOKOKO J
PUTSOANE AJ
HEARD:               04 FEBRUARY 2026
DELIVERED:      05 MARCH 2026
SUMMARY
Constitutional Law
: The Speaker of the National Assembly and others being sued to compel the National Assembly to fulfil its constitutional mandate to enact the law in terms of section 59(1)(e) after having voted against complying with this mandate - Held, whilst Parliament has power to vote on any question put to, it, however does not have power not to disobey the constitution through its mechanism of voting – The decision to jettison the efforts to comply with the constitution declared unconstitutional and, consequently, the National Assembly directed to act constitutionally.
ANNOTATIONS
Legislation:
Constitution of Lesotho 1993
National Assembly of Lesotho Standing Order,2022
Cases:
Lesotho
Christian Advocates and Ambassadors Association and Others v Speaker of the National Assembly and Others Constitutional Case No. 0018/2022)
England
Julius V The Lord Bishop of Oxford (1879-80) 5 AC 214 (HL)
South Africa
Capitec Bank Holdings Limited and Another V Coral Lagoon Investments 194 (Pty) Ltd and Others 2022 (1) SA 100 (SCA)
Doctors for Life International V Speaker of National Assembly and Others 2006(6) SA 416 (CC)
Economic Freedom Fighters V Speaker of the National Assembly and Others 2016 (3) SA 580 (CC)
President of the Republic of South Africa and Others V South African Rugby Football Union and Others 1999(4) SA 147(CC)
South African Police Service V Public Servants Association 2007 (3) SA 521 (CC)
Steenkamp and Others V Edcon Ltd 2016 (3) SA 251 (CC)
Schwartz V Schwartz 1984 (4) SA 467 (A)
Van Rooyen and Others V The State and Others (General Council of the Bar of South Africa Intervening) 2002 (5) SA 246 (CC)
JUDGMENT
MOKHESI J
[1]
Introduction
This case raises an important issue whether Parliament can legitimately defeat one section of the Constitution by complying with another. The answer to this anterior question naturally engages an interpretative exercise of the sections involved, by this court. This constitutional motion was brought by the party leader of Socialist Revolution and two other political parties which all have representation in the National Assembly.  In this motion, applicants are seeking to review the decision of the National Assembly to reject the privately brought bill known as “Conflict of Interest Bill, 2024” which was brought in terms of the provisions of Section 59(1)(e) of the Constitution of Lesotho 1993.  The reliefs sought are couched as follows:
“(a) A review, in terms of Section 119(1) of the Lesotho Constitution, 1993, that the Government and/or coalition of ruling parties’ conduct of casting majority votes against motion on the Bill paving way for passing of “Confliction of Interest “Legislation mandated by Section 59(1)(e) of the Constitution be reviewed and set aside as unconstitutional.
(b) A mandamus directing the 11
th
Parliament to promulgate legislation regulating “conflict of interest” in terms of Section 59(1)(e) of the Constitution, within one (1) year.”
[2]
Background
On 1
st
November 2024 a member of the National Assembly by the names of Dr Tšepo Lipholo moved a motion for leave to bring a private member’s bill known as ‘Conflict of Interest Bill, 2024’ as provided for by Standing Order No. 52(1).  The motion was seconded per the standard procedure of the National Assembly.  It was further debated by all members present, and by majority of the votes, the Bill was jettisoned.  The Motion was framed as follows:
“That this Honourable House gives leave to bring a private member’s Bill in terms of Standing Order No. 52(1) with long title Conflict of Interest Bill 2024:  A Bill for an Act to limit prohibit members of Parliament and the Executive to have certain prescribed interests in Government contracts that violate the letter and spirit of Section 59(1)(e) of the Constitution and as such would have the effect of disqualifying a member from being elected and/or continue to be a member of Senate and National Assembly; and the Bill to also deal with conflict of interest in respect of other spectrums of Government officials.”
[3]     The applicants were dissatisfied with this decision to reject the motion, and as a result brought the present constitutional motion seeking the reliefs outlined in the introductory part of this judgment.  In response, the Speaker of the National Assembly who is the 1
st
respondent raised a point in
limine
regarding the jurisdiction of this court to entertain this motion, the essence of which is to buttress the importance of the hallowed principle of our constitutional law pertaining to separation of powers between the Executive, Parliament and the Judiciary.  His argument impels a non-interventionist approach by the courts in the internal processes of the parliament especially where the internal rules of the National Assembly were followed, as in the present case, in rejecting a proposed bill.  This point was averred in the following manner: (at para. 4 of his answering affidavit):
“Points in limine
Jurisdiction
4.1 The applicants are in effect challenging the procedure in Parliament and in terms of section 81(1) of the Constitution each House of Parliament may regulate its own procedure and make rules for the orderly conduct of its own proceedings.
4.2 Where Parliament has failed to follow proper legislative procedures, the court can be called upon to review a decision of Parliament, and in the instant case, all procedures were followed in terms of national Assembly Standing Orders, 2022.  As a result, the Honourable Court has no jurisdiction to entertain this matter.
4.3 The principle of parliamentary sovereignty is enshrined in Lesotho, and parliament has the ultimate authority to decide which laws are passed or not.  This court does not have the power to compel parliament to pass a law or require it to approve private member’s bill.  The doctrine of separation of powers demands that the judiciary respects the independence and legislative authority of Parliament.”
[4]     Whether this court has jurisdiction to entertain this matter will resolve itself in light of the ensuing discussion as it is dependent on whether in terms of Section 59(1)(e) of the Constitution, a positive duty or obligation is cast upon Parliament to enact a law disqualifying a person from being elected as a member of the National Assembly or being nominated as a Senator or being designated to be a Senator in the stead of the Principal Chief, who has any interest in any government contract.  Both counsel were in agreement that if this court finds that Section 59(1)(e) places an obligation on Parliament to enact such a law a vote by the National Assembly rejecting a Bill would be unconstitutional.
[5]     It is trite that the ecosystem of the Judiciary, Parliament and Executive branches of the state is anchored on the important constitutional principle of separation of powers between these branches.  For present purposes, the relationship between the courts and the legislature was aptly articulated in the South African case
of
Doctors for Life International V Speaker of National Assembly and Others 2006(6) SA 416 (CC)
at paras. 36 – 38:
“36. Parliament has a very special role to play in our constitutional democracy – it is the principal legislative organ of the state. With due regard to that role, it must be free to carry out its functions without interference. To this extent, it has the power to “determine and control its internal arrangements, proceedings and procedures”. The business of Parliament might well be stalled while the question of what relief should be granted is argued out in the courts. Indeed, the parliamentary process would be paralysed if Parliament were to spend its time defending its legislative process in the courts. This would undermine one of the essential features of our democracy: the separation of powers.
37.The constitutional principle of separation of powers requires that other branches of government refrain from interfering in parliamentary proceedings. This principle is not simply an abstract notion; it is reflected in the very structure of our government. The structure of the provisions entrusting and separating powers between the legislative, executive and judicial branches reflects the concept of separation of powers. The principle “has important consequences for the way in which and the institutions by which power can be exercised.” Courts must be conscious of the vital limits on judicial authority and the Constitution’s design to leave certain matters to other branches of government. They too must observe the constitutional limits of their authority. This means that the judiciary should not interfere in the processes of other branches of government unless to do so is mandated by the Constitution.
38.But under our constitutional democracy, the Constitution is the supreme law. It is binding on all branches of government and no less on Parliament. When it exercises its legislative authority, Parliament “must act in accordance with, and within the limits of, the Constitution”, and the supremacy of the Constitution requires that “the obligations imposed by it must be fulfilled.” Courts are required by the Constitution “to ensure that all branches of government act within the law” and fulfil their constitutional obligations. This Court “has been given the responsibility of being the ultimate guardian of the Constitution and its values.” …. It would therefore require clear language of the Constitution to deprive this Court of its jurisdiction to enforce the Constitution.”
[6]     From this dictum three important principles can be extracted; Parliament is a critical and important legislative organ of the state which must be left alone to perform its functions undisturbed by unmeritorious legal challenges to how it conducts its legislative processes.  As stated earlier, the relationship between these co-equal branches of the state is anchored on the principle of separation of powers which sets parameters on how each branch should be left to enjoy its constitutional space unperturbed by another.  These limits, the courts must always be alive to and religiously respect unless interference is sourced from the constitution itself.  However, because the constitution is the supreme law of the land it binds all branches of the State without exception, and if there is a breach of the constitution by either the Executive or Parliament, the courts have ‘been given the responsibility of being the ultimate guardian of the Constitution and its values’
(President of the Republic of South Africa and Others V South African Rugby Football Union and Others 1999(4) SA 147(CC)
para.72).
[7]     To determine whether the National Assembly acted in conflict with the Constitution, an interpretative exercise on Sections 58 and  59(1)(e) of the Constitution should be undertaken, aided of course by the well-known canons of interpretation in the form of a triad of considerations, the language used, understood in the context in which it is used, and having regard to the purpose of the provision (
Capitec Bank Holdings Limited and Another V Coral Lagoon Investments 194 (Pty) Ltd and Others 2022 (1) SA 100 (SCA)
at para. 25).
[8]     Section 58 of the Constitution provides:
“
58. Qualifications for membership of Parliament.
Subject to the provisions of section 59 of this Constitution, a person shall be qualified to be nominated as a Senator by the King acting in accordance with the advice of the Council of State or designated by a Principal Chief as a Senator in his place if, and shall not be so qualified unless, at the date of his nomination or designation, he—
is a citizen of Lesotho; and
(b) is able to speak and, unless incapacitated by blindness or other physical cause, to read and write either the Sesotho or English languages well enough to take an active part in the proceedings of the Senate.
(2) Subject to the provisions of section 59 of this Constitution, a person shall be qualified to be elected as a member of the National Assembly if, and shall not be so qualified unless, at the date of his nomination for election, he—
is a citizen of Lesotho; and
(b) is registered in some constituency as an elector in elections to the National Assembly and is not disqualified from voting in such elections; and
(c) is able to speak and, unless incapacitated by blindness or other physical cause, to read and write either the Sesotho or English language well enough to take an active part in the proceedings of the National Assembly.
[9]     On the other hand Section 59 provides:
“
59. Disqualifications for membership of Parliament.
(1) No person shall be qualified to be nominated as a Senator by the King acting in accordance with the advice of the Council of State or designated by a Principal Chief as a Senator in his place and no person shall be qualified to be elected as a member of the National Assembly if, at the date of his nomination or designation or, as the case may be, at the date of his nomination for election, he—
is, by virtue of his own act, under any acknowledgement of allegiance, obedience or adherence to any foreign power or state; or (b) is under sentence of death imposed on him by any court in Lesotho; or
is under any law in force in Lesotho, adjudged or otherwise declared to be of unsound mind; or
is an unrehabilitated insolvent, having been adjudged or otherwise declared insolvent under any law in force in Lesotho; or
(e) subject to such exceptions and limitations as may be prescribed by Parliament, has any such interest in any such government contract as may be so prescribed.
(2) Parliament may provide that a person who, at the date of his nomination for election, holds or is acting in any office that is specified by Parliament and the functions of which involve responsibility for, or in connection with, the conduct of any election to the National Assembly or the compilation of any register of electors for the purposes of such an election shall not be qualified to be elected as a member of the National Assembly.
(3) Parliament may provide that a person who is convicted by any court of any offence that is prescribed by Parliament and that is connected with the election of members of the National Assembly or who is reported guilty of such an offence by the court trying an election petition shall not be qualified to be nominated for election as a member of the National Assembly for such period (not exceeding five years) following his conviction or, as the case may be, following the report of the court as may be so prescribed.
(4) Parliament may provide that, subject to such exceptions and limitations as may be prescribed by Parliament, a person shall not be qualified to be nominated as a Senator or elected as a member of the National Assembly if—
(a) he holds or acts in any office or appointment that is so prescribed; or (b) he is a member of the Defence Force; or
(c) he is a member of the Police Force; or
(d) he is a member of the National Security Service: or
(e) he is a member of the Prison Service.
(5) No person shall be qualified to be elected as a member of the National Assembly who, at the date of his nomination for election as such a member, is a Principal Chief or is otherwise a Senator.
(6) In subsection (1)(e) “government contract” means any contract made with the Government of Lesotho or with a department of that Government or with an officer of that Government contracting as such.”
[10]   These two sections deal with distinct but interrelated subject matters: Section 58 deals with the qualification of persons to be nominated as Senators by the King or designation of a person to be a Senator in the stead of the Principal Chief and qualification of persons to be elected as a member of the National Assembly. In terms of section 58, in a case of a Senator, a person qualifies to be nominated as such by the King or be designated to be a Senator in the stead of the Principal Chief, if he is the citizen of Lesotho and is able to speak and unless he is incapacitated by blindness or other physical cause to read and write either the Sesotho or English languages well enough to participate in the proceedings of the Senate. The same conditions have been imposed on the person wishing to be elected to the National Assembly with an additional condition being that he/she must be registered in some constituency as an elector in elections to the National Assembly and is not disqualified from voting in such elections.  These qualifications are subject to the provisions of Section 59 which deals with disqualifications for membership of Parliament.  In essence, what Section 59 does is to disqualify any person from being nominated as a Senator or being elected as a member of the National Assembly if he/she falls within its proscription reach even if he/she meets the requirements for election, nomination or designation provided under section 58.
[11]   The first four disqualifying factors under Section 59(1) present no problem, except for the last factor under sub-section (1)(e) which is the subject of the present proceedings. This last one engenders heated contestations because it is subject to diametrically opposed interpretations by both side to the present litigation. What engenders these contestations is the word
‘may’
as used in the sub-paragraph.  The Speaker argues that the use of this word signifies an intention on the part of the Constitution to give Parliament a discretion whether to enact the law providing for disqualification to be nominated or elected to Parliament, on account of such a person’s interest in a government contract.  He argues further that Section 75(1) of the Constitution provides that any question proposed for decision in either house of Parliament shall be determined by a majority votes of the members present and voting; that in the present instance the National Assembly voted against the Bill, and that should be the end of the matter.
[12]   On the other hand the applicants contend that the word
“may”
as used in the sub-paragraph should be interpreted to mean
“shall”,
thereby imposing an obligation on Parliament to enact the law disqualifying persons from being elected as members of the National Assembly or being nominated to the Senate, based on their interest in the government contract.  They argue that if this court is with them that the sub-paragraph imposes an obligation to enact such a law, the court should find that in voting against the Bill the National Assembly acted unconstitutionally.
[13]   Earlier under paragraph [3] of this judgment I reproduced the Speaker’s averments from his answering affidavit lamenting the involvement of the courts in the internal matters of Parliament. I did that in part to show that the respondents misunderstood the applicants’ case.  The applicants’ case is not that the internal rules or procedures were not followed when the Bill was rejected.  It is rather that the National Assembly by majority vote disobeyed the dictates of the Constitution.  Put differently, the National Assembly refused to obey the injunction of the Constitution to enact the law regulating these matters.
[14]   The use of the word
“may”
in a statute generally connotes that whoever is bestowed with power is also given a choice on how to use it.  But its use may not always be decisive just like the use of the words
“shall”
and
“must”
(Steenkamp and Others V Edcon Ltd 2016 (3) SA 251 (CC)
at para. 182).  In each case therefore, it is important to properly construe a provision to establish its purpose (
Steenkamp and Others
case
ibid
para. 182).  There may, however, be instances where a statute may grant power with a duty to exercise it.  This was recognised more than a century ago in
Julius V The Lord Bishop of Oxford (1879-80) 5 AC 214 (HL)
at 222-223 when Cairns LC stated:
“There may be something in the nature of the thing empowered to be done, something in the object for which it is done, something in the conditions under which it is to be done, something in the title of the person or persons for whose benefit the power is to be exercised, which may couple the power with a duty, and make it the duty of the person in whom power is reposed to exercise that power when called upon to do so”.
[15]   These sentiments were expressed regarding permissive language in a statute in
Schwartz V Schwartz 1984 (4) SA 467 (A)
at 473-474:
“A statutory enactment conferring a power in permissive language may nevertheless have to be construed as making it the duty of the person or authority in whom the power is reposed to exercise that power when conditions prescribed as justifying its exercise have been satisfied.  Whether an enactment should be so construed depend on, inter alia, the language in which it is couched, the context in which it appears, the general scope and object of the legislation, the nature of the thing empowered to be done and the person or persons for whose benefit the power is to be exercised.”
[16]   In the case of
Van Rooyen and Others V The State and Others (General Council of the Bar of South Africa Intervening) 2002 (5) SA 246 (CC) (
see also
South African Police Service V Public Servants Association 2007 (3) SA 521 (CC)
at paras. 16 of
Sachs J’s judgement and Jacoob J’s judgment at para.38
)
it was stated that the word
“may”
be construed in two ways: firstly, either to give a complete discretion to the repository of power or, secondly, to bestow power coupled with a duty to exercise it.
[17]   Section 81(1) of the Constitution empowers each House of Parliament to regulate its own procedure and to make rules for the orderly conduct of their own proceedings.  It is in line with this provision that the National Assembly passed National Assembly Standing Order No. 34, which provides that every motion moved must be seconded; that the Speaker shall not put a question on a motion which is not seconded, and that when a motion has been moved and seconded, the Speaker or Chairperson shall propose a question thereon to the House or the committee in the same terms and thereupon the question will be debated;  that when there are no more members wishing to speak, the Speaker or Chairperson shall put a question to the House or the committee for its decision.  Regarding the Bill in question, this procedure was followed, and the decision of the majority, by votes, was to reject it.  To that extend there is no debate, because in terms of Standing Order No. 45(1) which was made pursuant to Section 75(1) of the Constitution, all questions put to the House shall be decided by the majority of votes of the members present and voting.
[18]   Evidently, the Constitution has set its face against persons being made to serve as Senators or being elected as members of the National Assembly, who acknowledge allegiance, obedience or adherence to any foreign State; is under sentence of death imposed on him by any court in Lesotho; is under any law in Lesotho adjudged or declared to be of unsound mind; is an unrehabilitated insolvent, having been declared as such under any law in Lesotho; or has any such interest in any such government contract as
may be
so prescribed subject to such exceptions and limitations as may be prescribed by Parliament.
[19]   Since the advent of this Constitution in 1993, the disqualifying factor under Section 59(1)(e) has not been effective.  The reason for this is that all the successive Parliaments since 1993 have not enacted it into law.  This takes us to the anterior question which has to be answered in this case.  Was Parliament obligated by the constitution to enact this disqualifying factor into law?  The answer to this question should be in the affirmative.  The use of the words
“may be”
in the sub-section are located in the context in which the Constitution sets out the factors which disqualify the would-be Senators or Members of the National Assembly, but it does so by listing all the disqualifying factors whilst leaving the last one – which we are concerned with in this case - to Parliament to enact into law and if it so wishes, to build into it exceptions and limitations or totally ban anyone from being so nominated as a Senator or being elected as a member of the National Assembly if he/she has interest in any government contract.
[20]   It is not difficult to fathom why the Constitution would set its face against anyone ascending to such high public offices when they have commercial interests in government: owing to human natural inclination for self-preservation, anyone who has a commercial interest in government would seek to unjustly protect them through unscrupulous means such as policy manipulation or other means.  Such persons may also develop contractual relationship with the government whilst they sit seat at its helm, and this could be a situation where these persons end up occupying the positions as ministers and prime ministers. Once they are in these executive positions, it is axiomatic that they wield substantial political and executive powers. The possibility that they may abuse their positions for personal gain looms ever so large. It is therefore, in light of the possible occurrence of these vices that Section 59(1)(e), should be seen.  Once the constitution disqualifies a person in this manner, by implication, it would therefore discourage anyone of these persons once they are elected to have commercial relationship with the government in which they occupy such influential positions.
[21]   The dominant theme under Section 59(1) is the disqualification of any person to be nominated or designated as a Senator or to be elected to the National Assembly if he/she has any such interest in any such government contract “subject to such exceptions and limitations as may be prescribed by Parliament.”  The use of the word
“may”
though in its usual sense connotes that whoever is endowed with power is also given a choice on how to use it, in the context in which it is used and the object of subsection, in my view, it should be construed as making it a duty on Parliament to enact legislation debarring a person from being nominated or designated as a Senator or being elected to the National Assembly if she/he has any interest in any government contract.  The structure of Section 59 is telling in this regard; Subsection (1) is couched in the usual mandatory terms.  It says,
“No person shall be qualified ...”
and it goes on to list the factors which disqualify a person.  Five factors are listed, but the last one is left to Parliament to enact the law which in its wisdom may be subject to exceptions and limitations.
[22]   To construe the use of this word in its usual sense leads to a situation where the purpose of subsection (1) is subverted because it would mean that Parliament is effectively limiting the list of disqualifying factors, if, as the Speaker argues, it is up to Parliament to decide whether to enact the law regulating the eligibility of the aspirant Senators or members of the National Assembly based on their private-public commercial interest.  If the Constitution had intended the word
‘may’
to retain its usual connotation it would have done as it did under Section 59(2), (3) and (4).  Under these subsections, it is left to Parliament to enact these proscriptions. When the constitution sets its face against a particular vice in this section it uses a categorical and commanding language as in subsections (1) and (5).  In the latter subsection it provides that
“No person shall be qualified to be elected as a member of the National Assembly who, at the date of his nomination for election as such a member, is a Principal Chief or is otherwise a Senator.”
It will be observed that the Constitution does not mandate or envisage an absolute disqualification if a person has any interest in a government contract.  It gives Parliament power to set out exceptions and limitations as it may find necessary, but what it does not have a discretion to do is not to enact the law in terms of Section 59(1)(e).
[23]   I turn to deal with the interesting contention which was advanced by the Speaker and advanced orally during argument before the court.  In his averments he states:
“3.5 In terms of standing order 34, every motion moved must be seconded.  Once it has been moved and seconded, the Speaker shall propose the question to the House and such question may be debated.
3.6 It is equally important to state that in terms of Standing Order 45(1) all questions put to the House shall be decided by the majority votes of the members present and voting.  And this is also in line with Section 75(1) of the Constitution.
3.7 The court will take cognizance of the fact that a private member moved a motion for Conflict of Interest Bill, and such motion as a standard procedure was seconded.  The Motion was further debated also as a standard procedure and all members present voted and majority negated the motion.
3.8 It is imperative to state therefore at the onset that the rules of parliament regarding motion were followed to the letter.”
[24]   The Speaker’s averments though straightforward in their thrust and purport, are symptomatic of a profound misapprehension under which Parliament operated on the day they jettisoned the motion in question. The misapprehension is that Parliament can refuse to do what it is obligated to do by the Constitution.  Put differently, and in a form of a question, having concluded above that in terms of Section 59(1)(e) Parliament was obligated to enact the law regulating disqualification of persons from being elected as members of Parliament or being nominated or designated as Senators when they have interest in government contracts, can Parliament legally vote not to comply with this constitutional obligation?  The answer should be in the negative as I demonstrate in the ensuing discussion.
[25]   Section 75(1) of the Constitution provides:
“Save as otherwise provided in this Constitution, any question proposed for decision in either House of Parliament shall be determined by a majority of the votes of the members present and voting.”
[26]   The right of Parliament to decide any question by majority of votes in terms of Section 75(1) should therefore be narrowly construed in light of other constitutional provisions that are materially relevant to it
(Economic Freedom Fighters V Speaker of the National Assembly and Others 2016 (3) SA 580 (CC)
at para. 17).  The use of the phrase “
save as otherwise provided in this Constitution,”
makes it clear that under normal circumstances a decision on the question proposed in either House of Parliament shall be determined by a majority of the votes unless a contrary method is otherwise provided elsewhere in the Constitution.  Where the Constitution places an obligation on Parliament to do something, it cannot vote to defeat the fulfilment of such an obligation.  The present case is a clear example of a situation where the Constitution has placed an obligation on Parliament to enact the law, but the National Assembly instead used voting mechanism to defeat it. That it cannot do.  Put differently, Parliament cannot vote to defeat the fulfilment of a constitutional obligation to enact the law under Section 59(1)(e).
[27]   Whilst members of Parliament are free to debate the structure and the content of such a Bill, they are, however, not free to scupper its enactment or to refuse to enact it, because to do so, they will be acting unconstitutionally.  The right of Parliament to decide a question put before it cannot be used to defeat its obligation to do what the Constitution has instructed it to do. To construe the power of Parliament to decide by majority vote on any question put will lead to a situation such as the present where the National Assembly voted to disobey the Constitution.  It does not have a right to vote to disobey the Constitution but rather it has a right to decide the issue which constitutionally falls within its purview.  As already seen when Parliament acts unconstitutionally the courts have a constitutional power to declare its conduct as unconstitutional and to consequentially direct it to act constitutionally (See
Christian Advocates and Ambassadors Association and Others v Speaker of the National Assembly and Others Constitutional Case No. 0018/2022).
[28]
Prolixity in affidavits.
Courts frown upon prolixity in affidavits.  In this matter the unnecessary and repetitive material was included in the applicants’ founding papers. A lot of argumentative material made its way into the founding affidavit, for example an argument about members of parliament violating their oath under Section 71 of the Constitution.  The founding affidavit is also replete with examples of irrelevant materials being included in it such as quoting the Bible and tabulating the names of the members of the National Assembly who voted against the Bill.  I do not understand what this tabulation serves to do especially when it is common cause that the majority of the members of the National Assembly voted against the Bill.
[29]   In the
Minister of Environmental Affairs & Tourism V Phambili Fisheries; Minister of Environmental Affairs & Tourism V Bato Star Fishing (Pty) Ltd 2003 (6) SA 407 (SCA)
at 439 para. 39
in the context of a replying affidavit which was unduly long and worthless, the court stated that
“being forced to wade through almost endless repetition brings about irritation, not persuasion.  It is time that the courts declare war on unnecessary prolix replying affidavits and upon those who inflate them.”
And in
Van Zyl and Others v Government of the Republic of South Africa and Others 2008 (3) SA 294 (SCA)
at 307 paras. 44-46,
in the context of an appeal against an order striking out parts of the affidavit Harms ADP stated that rather than wasting its time analysing such affidavit
“sentence by sentence and paragraph by paragraph such affidavits should be struck out as a whole” mero motu
by the court.  Prolixity in affidavit is quite an endemic problem in this court and should be nipped in the bud.  Counsel are therefore warned that in the not-too-distant future, appropriate sanctions will be meted out on counsel who are responsible for preparing these affidavits and attaching irrelevant annexures.
[30]
Costs
There is no reason why costs should not follow the result in this matter nor has an argument been advanced for such an outcome by the respondents’ counsel.
[31]   In the result the following order is made:
The application succeeds with costs on the following terms:
The Government and/or coalition of ruling parties’ conduct of casting majority votes against motion on the Bill paving way for passing of “Conflict of Interest” legislation mandated by Section 59(1)(e) of the Constitution is reviewed and set aside as unconstitutional.
Parliament is directed to promulgate legislation in terms of Section 59(1)(e) of the Constitution within 24 months of the delivery of this judgment.
